paper_metadata.title: Denial and distrust: explaining the partisan climate gap
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Non-probability Prolific survey of self-identified Democrats/Republicans (n=1808), collected Mar 22–30, 2021.
exact_tax_terms_used: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Respondents indicated support/oppose for an explicit carbon tax item; Kitagawa–Blinder–Oaxaca decompositions (Table 3, carbon-tax column) estimate how the Republicans–Democrats support gap is explained by trust in science, climate-science denial, and (smaller) trust in government.

determinants.definition: Trust in the scientific community on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
determinants.mechanism: Higher trust in scientific expertise increases perceived credibility/legitimacy of climate claims, raising willingness to support policies like a carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): trust(science) = −0.13*** (SE ≈ 0.02). Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
determinants.notes: Coefficient is negative because the decomposed gap is defined as (Republicans − Democrats). Substantively, higher trust (more common among Democrats) aligns with higher carbon-tax support.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Climate-science denial / low acceptance of climate evidence
determinants.definition: Beliefs about whether climate change is occurring, combining yes/no and certainty; operationalized as climate-science denial in the decomposition.
determinants.mechanism: More denial/less acceptance reduces perceived need for mitigation and lowers support for a carbon tax designed to reduce fossil-fuel use.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): climate denial = −0.12*** (SE ≈ 0.02). Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
determinants.notes: Sign reflects the decomposed gap orientation (Republicans minus Democrats). Substantively, more denial (more common among Republicans) corresponds to lower carbon-tax support.

determinants.definition: Trust in government on issues related to climate change (definitely/probably/not sure/probably not/definitely not).
determinants.mechanism: Higher trust in government increases confidence that climate policy would be implemented effectively and with legitimacy, supporting carbon-tax approval.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: A carbon tax on energy companies and utilities that will raise the cost of using fossil fuels and incentivize use of renewable energy sources.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for the carbon tax policy (support vs oppose/not sure).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Endowment coefficient (Republicans − Democrats gap): trust(gov’t) = −0.01**. Total explained by observed covariates for carbon tax = 54.57%.
determinants.notes: Negative sign reflects (Republicans − Democrats) gap definition; substantively, greater government trust aligns with higher carbon-tax support, though the effect is small.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Political control. For the carbon tax column in Table 3, the coefficient is reported as ~0.00 (no clear significance), so an acceptability direction is not identifiable.
Included in models but omitted from Table 3 for interpretation; authors state minor explanatory power for certain policies without specifying carbon tax specifically in the provided excerpt.
